title: Copper Breaks $11,000 per Tonne for First Time Since 2024 on Deficit Fears
description: Copper Breaks $11,000 per Tonne for First Time Since 2024 on Deficit Fears Wall Street Journal
htmlContent:
Copper Breaks $11,000 per Tonne for First Time Since 2024 on Deficit Fears

By Rhiannon Hoyle, The Wall Street Journal • June 8, 2026

LONDON – Copper surpassed $11,000 per tonne on the London Metal Exchange on Monday for the first time since the spring of 2024, as the confluence of shrinking warehouse inventories, supply disruptions in Chile and unrelenting demand from artificial intelligence infrastructure projects pushed the metal to a new multi-year milestone.

LME three-month copper reached $11,043 per tonne in early European trading before easing slightly to $10,978 by midday. COMEX copper futures in New York also crossed the psychologically important $5.00-per-pound threshold for the first time since 2024.

The move was driven by a deteriorating physical supply picture. LME warehouse stocks have fallen for 15 consecutive sessions to stand at 76,450 tonnes, equivalent to less than 18 hours of global consumption. Cancelled warrants — a leading indicator of further withdrawals — rose to represent 42% of total LME stocks, pointing to continued drawdowns in the near term.

"The market is in a genuine state of scarcity," said Michael Widmer, head of metals research at Bank of America. "What is unusual about this cycle is that the demand side is not only strong; it has a structural, technology-driven underpinning that shows no sign of slowing. Meanwhile, supply is barely growing. This is the recipe for a sustained bull market."

The AI data centre buildout remains the dominant demand narrative. Technology groups including Microsoft, Amazon, Google and Meta have collectively committed to spending well over $300 billion on cloud and AI infrastructure in 2026 and 2027, with each dollar of that investment generating roughly 30 grams of copper demand, according to Citigroup estimates.

Supply headwinds continue to mount. Strikes and go-slows at Chilean mines, declining ore grades at Codelco's legacy operations, and permitting delays at several large development projects in the United States and Australia have all contributed to a widening gap between supply and demand.

The International Copper Study Group's most recent monthly bulletin estimated the global copper market deficit at 58,000 tonnes for April alone, putting the year-to-date deficit at approximately 290,000 tonnes — on track to exceed 600,000 tonnes for the full year if current trends hold.

Several major investment banks have now raised their year-end copper price targets above $11,500 per tonne, with Goldman Sachs targeting $12,000 and Citigroup projecting a potential spike to $13,000 if supply disruptions worsen into the second half. Bull market momentum is also drawing in speculative funds; net long positioning on COMEX copper futures rose to its highest level since late 2024 in Friday's Commitments of Traders report.

Some analysts cautioned that a Chinese economic slowdown or an unexpected resolution of the Chilean strikes could trigger a sharp correction, but the prevailing mood in metal markets on Monday was one of bullish conviction about the structural demand thesis.

Tags: copper, $11000, LME, COMEX, deficit, AI, data center, Chile, Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, Citigroup

Sentiment signal: bullish
